burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost an eight-year-old on her birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Elizabeth's third child Abigail Hooker Talcott was born April 15, 1848 and died on her eighth birthday, April 15, 1856. The Henry Talcott household lost this daughter in West Hartford the same year his elder brother Sam IV's daughter Sarah Whiting Talcott had also died at thirteen, only five years earlier.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost an eight-year-old on her birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Elizabeth's third child Abigail Hooker Talcott was born April 15, 1848 and died on her eighth birthday, April 15, 1856. The Henry Talcott household lost this daughter in West Hartford the same year his elder brother Sam IV's daughter Sarah Whiting Talcott had also died at thirteen, only five years earlier.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-three years across two centuries
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth was born in 1819, the year before Connecticut adopted its second state constitution. She married Henry Talcott in August 1841, bore him six children, and was widowed in 1888 at sixty-eight. She lived another twenty-five years to 1913, dying at ninety-three — outliving her husband by a quarter-century.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name almost certainly honors Rev. Francis Wayland (1796-1865), the prominent Baptist minister, philosopher, and longtime president of Brown University, whose moral and educational works were widely read in 1840s New England Congregationalist households.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave 59310746.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the Brown University president philosopher
narrative_blocks.body: Francis Wayland was born in 1842, ten years before Rev. Francis Wayland retired from his twenty-eight-year presidency at Brown University and three years before Wayland's most influential book on moral philosophy went into its tenth printing. The Talcott household clearly read Baptist intellectual material despite its own Hooker-Congregationalist heritage.

career.notes: Farmer at West Hartford CT until his death in 1893. Per EH 1909 entry 3210.

parents.father_research_notes: Libni Hall. Per EH 1909 entry 3210; not built per Jalapeño.
parents.mother_research_notes: Eliza Brown Hall. Per EH 1909 entry 3210; not built per Jalapeño.

narrative_blocks.header: Two children with the West Hartford farmer
narrative_blocks.body: Emma Amelia Hall married Francis Wayland Talcott of West Hartford on October 16, 1877 when she was twenty-five. She bore him two children: Herbert Wayland (1879) and Mabel Hall (1884). Francis died in 1893; Emma's death date and burial place are not yet documented.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his father Francis Wayland Talcott's middle name.

narrative_blocks.header: Born to a forty-two-year-old farmer father
narrative_blocks.body: Herbert Wayland Talcott was born in West Hartford in October 1879 — the elder of two children. His father Francis Wayland Talcott was forty-two when Herbert was born and farmed in West Hartford until his death in 1893, when Herbert was fourteen and his sister Mabel was eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Confused with her father's wife in some sources
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Talcott Hall was the daughter of Francis Wayland Talcott (1842-1893), born October 3, 1884 — when her father was forty-two. Some genealogical sources incorrectly listed her as Francis's wife rather than his daughter. She lived seventy-one years and married a man surnamed Hall.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Vassar College — early generation of women's college graduates.

narrative_blocks.header: Vassar graduate in the college's first decade
narrative_blocks.body: Vassar College opened in Poughkeepsie New York in 1865 as one of the first American institutions to grant degrees to women. Eliza Sophia Talcott — born 1846 in West Hartford — graduated as part of that founding generation of women's higher education.

naming_inspiration.description: Given her grandmother Abigail Pantry Hooker's name in tribute.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on her eighth birthday in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail was born April 15, 1848 and named for her paternal grandmother Abigail Pantry Hooker. She died on her eighth birthday — April 15, 1856 — in West Hartford. Cause of death not specified in available sources.

burial.plot_notes: Fairview Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave 148056026.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived one hundred and two years
narrative_blocks.body: Emeline was born October 10, 1849 in Hartford and died February 25, 1952 in West Hartford at one hundred and two — across the entire span from the California Gold Rush and the construction of the Hartford streetcar system to the Korean War and the early Cold War. One of the longest-lived entries in this tree.

burial.plot_notes: Fairview Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave 148056069.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried her mother's maiden name as middle
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Whiting Talcott was given her mother Elizabeth Wells Whiting Talcott's maiden surname as her middle name — an explicit tribute to the Whiting family line. Born in West Hartford in 1852, she lived eighty-four years there and was buried at Fairview Cemetery alongside her sister Emeline.

compiler_connection.notes: Samuel Talcott's marriage to Abigail Pantry Hooker is the key Talcott-Hooker intersection in the compiler's maternal line. Their son Seth (H01789) → John Butler (H03203) → Florence → Helen → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: T01389 Samuel × HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John B. → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

primary_residence.notes: Resided in Eastbury (Glastonbury) until 1788, then West Hartford for the rest of his life.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with wife H00687 Abigail Pantry Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 he married Abigail Pantry Hooker in West Hartford. He was a fifth-generation Talcott; she was a fifth-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved from Glastonbury to West Hartford in 1788
narrative_blocks.body: He left the Talcott homeland in Eastbury for West Hartford seven years before his marriage. He stayed there for the rest of his life.

classification.lineage_note: Thomas¹ → Samuel²(H00007) → John³ → Roger⁴ → Thomas⁵ → Abigail⁶ Pantry Hooker
classification.intersection_note: Descends from Thomas Hooker (paternal) AND marries into Talcott line (T01389). Compiler descends from her through Talcott line.

compiler_connection.notes: Abigail Pantry Hooker is the compiler's generation 6 ancestor on the maternal Talcott line. Her marriage to Samuel Talcott (T01389) on Jan 25, 1795 is the Hooker-Talcott intersection that gives the compiler his middle name 'Talcott'. Her son Seth Talcott (H01789) → John Butler Talcott (H03203) → Florence H. Talcott → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker × T01389 Samuel Talcott → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

open_questions.question: EH entry number for Abigail Pantry Hooker
open_questions.notes: Talcott 1876 identifies her as 5th generation from Thomas Hooker. EH entry number unknown — currently HD0064. Once found, rename to H#####.
open_questions.action: Search EH for children of Thomas Hart Hooker (who held Skene prisoners, LM005); find Abigail Pantry Hooker entry

primary_residence.notes: Born West Hartford; lived there with husband Samuel Talcott after 1788; died there 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 she married Samuel Talcott of Glastonbury. She was a fifth-generation Hooker; he was a fifth-generation Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father's freed man left her his estate
narrative_blocks.body: Bristow had been freed by her father Thomas Hart Hooker at the news of Lexington in April 1775. His 1791 will bequeathed his estate to her and her brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died at Roxbury in the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: He freed his enslaved man Bristow at news of the Battle of Lexington and enlisted with a Farmington volunteer company. He sickened and died near Boston in winter 1775-76, buried at Roxbury.

narrative_blocks.header: Eighty-four years in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: She was born and died in West Hartford, where she stayed for the entirety of her marriage and widowhood. She is buried in Old Center Cemetery beside her husband.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with husband T01389 Samuel Talcott III.

bio.notes: 'Pantry' appears to be a middle name, possibly a family name. EH entry number unknown — using HD prefix until confirmed.

compiler_connection.notes: T01001 Samuel is the compiler's gen 4 Talcott ancestor. His son T01389 Samuel married Abigail Pantry Hooker — the key Hooker-Talcott intersection.
compiler_connection.path: T01001 → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

notable.notable_blurb: Great-grandfather of Adm. George Dewey of Manila Bay

cross_connections.display_label: Both buried in rubble of the 1766 schoolhouse explosion

military_service.notes: FindAGrave Old Eastbury memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' Active in the Revolutionary War period; died Glastonbury CT 16 March 1780, mid-war, leaving his fifteen-year-old son Samuel (T01389) without a father.

burial.plot_notes: Old Eastbury Cemetery, Glastonbury CT. FindAGrave 61929162. Stone surviving intact.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War colonel from Glastonbury
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as a Connecticut militia Colonel in the Revolutionary War — the rank by which his Old Eastbury Cemetery memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' He was the third generation of Talcott military service, his father Capt. Samuel having served in the Connecticut militia of the colonial era and his ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott having commanded Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield in 1690.

narrative_blocks.header: Four children by Mary, none by Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Mary Smith of Hartford in 1757; she bore him Mary, Hannah, Samuel, and Capt. Nehemiah before her 1770 death at thirty-seven. He remarried Sarah (surname not preserved); she had no children with him and outlived him.

narrative_blocks.header: Died mid-Revolution leaving two young sons
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel died in Glastonbury on 16 March 1780 at age 47, squarely in the middle of the Revolutionary War he had served. Sons Samuel and Nehemiah were just fifteen and thirteen at his death; older daughters Mary and Hannah were already grown. His Glastonbury gravestone survives intact at Old Eastbury Cemetery: 'In Memory of Mr. Samuel Talcott who departed this life March 16th, 1780 Aged 47 years.'

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from the Deerfield dragoon commander
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott (b.1635) commanded the Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield Massachusetts at the outbreak of King William's War in 1690. That ancestor's father had served as deputy governor of Connecticut Colony in 1637 and as the colony's treasurer through the 1650s. Three generations of Talcott civic and military service stand between Col. Samuel and the colony's founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of the Manila Bay admiral
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's great-grandson Admiral George Dewey commanded the U.S. fleet at the Battle of Manila Bay in May 1898, sinking all eight Spanish ships with no American deaths and only eight wounded. The Talcott military line continued forward from Samuel's Revolutionary service through three generations to one of the most decisive naval victories in American history.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived the 1766 Hartford schoolhouse explosion
narrative_blocks.body: On 23 May 1766 he was among twenty-eight men buried in the rubble when the Hartford schoolhouse exploded — gunpowder ignited during the town's Stamp Act repeal celebration. He survived with severe burns to his face and arms; six others, including Levi Jones, did not.

birth.note: Per family source: 'Mary was born in 1733 in Hartford, CT'

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Held British Loyalist prisoners here during the Revolution.

notable.notable_blurb: Freed enslaved Bristow before enlisting; Siege of Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Died of pleurisy at Boston — unmarked grave
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the first to enlist at Farmington in 1775 and joined the army near Boston. He died of pleurisy on November 26, 1775, age 30, during the Siege of Boston — buried in an unmarked grave at Roxbury. EH wrote: "Today the ashes of those patriot dead rest peacefully beneath the wild turmoil of Boston's streets."

narrative_blocks.header: Freed Bristow days before enlisting — £60 paid
narrative_blocks.body: On May 9, 1775 — days before leaving for war — he signed a Deed of Manumission freeing Bristow, an African man age 44, who paid £60 (roughly 400 days' wages) for his freedom. The deed reads: "he may go & come when & wherever he pleases." Bristow later willed his estate to Thomas's children. A West Hartford middle school was named Bristow Middle School in 2005 — believed to be the first Connecticut school named for a formerly enslaved person.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother Roger survived — rose to Major
narrative_blocks.body: He and his half-brother Roger Hooker — different mothers; Roger's was Anna Kellogg, his was Mercy Hart — both answered the Revolution's first call. Thomas died of disease in 1775; Roger rose to Major, carried news of Burgoyne's surrender to the British, and outlived the war by 55 years.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Abigail Pantry Hooker married Samuel Talcott, uniting the Hooker and Talcott founding-family lines of Hartford. This is the compiler's primary point of double descent from Thomas Hooker and the Talcott family simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: West Hartford home now a National Register site
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hart built his West Hartford home in 1770 — the Sarah Whitman Hooker Homestead at the corner of South Main Street and New Britain Avenue, where his widow Sarah continued to live and raise their children after his death. The house stands on the National Register of Historic Places, named for her.

documents.document_blurb: Summons to appear before CT General Assembly, Woodruff v. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.unit: Suffolk County Militia, Massachusetts Militia; Connecticut volunteers from Farmington
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call 1775. "One of the first to enlist." Joined army near Boston; died of pleurisy Nov 26, 1775 during Siege of Boston. Buried in unmarked grave at Roxbury MA.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hart" commemorates his mother Mercy Hart

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather succeeded Rev. Samuel Hooker at Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather Rev. Samuel Whitman succeeded Rev. Samuel Hooker as minister at Farmington — making her family already entwined with the Hooker line before her marriage. Rev. Samuel Whitman's mother Anna Alcock was a granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker's sister.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed in 1775 — remarried four years later
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Thomas Hart Hooker died of pleurisy during the Siege of Boston on November 26, 1775. On April 8, 1779 she married Capt. Seth Collins of West Hartford as his second wife. She died at West Hartford on June 5, 1837.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: She lived here with first husband Thomas Hart Hooker, then with second husband Capt. Seth Collins.

burialCemetery.name: Old Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Burial site of T01389 Samuel Talcott III, H00687 Abigail Pantry Hooker, H01790 Mary Talcott, H01792 Henry Talcott. West Hartford CT.

INST037.name: Vassar College
INST037.notes: Founded 1861 Poughkeepsie NY. One of the original Seven Sisters colleges. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Lucy Maria Edwards Remy (HD2224), daughter of John Stark Edwards (HD2183), granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards Jr. and great-great-great-granddaughter of Thomas Hooker, attended Vassar. Her obituary (Indianapolis newspaper, Dec 31, 1925) notes she attended the college and was "regarded as a woman of high intellectual attainments." Also: Louise Tayler-Jones (, Wellesley 1896 — though she is NOT a Hooker descendant, being from the second Tayler marriage).
